rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE IN RE: RICHARD YOUNG 2025 TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Defendant Respondent Cross-Petitioner Elisa Calica'S Cross-Petition And Quash Summons And Cross-Petition
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2 Motion to Strike/Motion to Quash] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Petitioner Tina Dykas's and Respondents Martha Ly and Donna Lew's Amended Motion to Strike Defendant/Respondent/Cross-Petitioner Elisa Calica's Cross-Petition and Quash Summons and Cross-Petition ("Motion to Strike" and "Motion to Quash"). The case Respondents cite in the Reply, Parsons v. Superior Ct. (2007) 149 Cal. App. 4th Supp. 1, 5 to argue they are specially appearing because they have simultaneously filed a demurrer and a motion to quash service, arose from defective service of summons in an unlawful detainer action, and the portion of the opinion Respondents rely on was overruled by Borsuk v. App. Div. of Superior Ct. (2015) 242 Cal. App. 4th 607. Further, as discussed below, there is no service of summons required on the Cross-Petition as currently pleaded. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 1014, "A defendant appears in an action when the defendant answers, demurs, files a notice of motion to strike[.]" Respondents Dykas, Ly and Lew have filed both a demurrer and a motion to strike. Dykas is the initial Petitioner in this matter. The court finds Dykas, Ly, and Lew have now appeared in this matter. On July 7, 2025, Elisa Calica filed Amended Cross-Petition 1. Disinheritance of an Heir Under California Probate Code 259 et seq; Declaratory Relief; 2. Isolation of an Elder Under Cal. W&I Code 15610.43; 3. Elder Financial Abuse; 4. Elder Physical Abuse and Neglect; 5. Recovery of Real and Personal Property to be Declared Part of Trust Pursuant to Probate Code 850; 6. Negligence/Negligence Per Se; 8. [sic] Indemnity ("Cross-Petition"). On July 14, 2025, Amendment to Amended Cross-Petition to Include Verification was filed. The Motion to Strike and Motion to Quash were filed as a single pleading on July 17, 2025. Per the Notice of Motion, movants seek an order striking portions of the Amended Cross-Petition under Code of Civil Procedure section 435(b)(1) for failure to verify the Amended Cross-Petition, specifically paragraphs 10, 11, 23, 25 of the Cross-Petition and paragraphs 6, 7, and 10 of the Prayer. Movants also seek an order quashing service of summons and Cross-Petition for lack of jurisdiction under Code of Civil Procedure section 418.10 due to failure to personally serve the summons and Cross-Petition on each Respondent. Motion to Strike specific paragraphs of the Cross-Petition pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 435(b)(1) for failure to verify the Amended Cross-Petition is DENIED AS MOOT. On July 14, 2025, Amendment to Amended Cross-Petition to Include Verification was filed. Motion to Quash service of summons and complaint for lack of jurisdiction under Code of Civil Procedure section 418.10 due to failure to personally serve the summons and complaint on each Respondent is DENIED. Civil action rules are applicable in Probate proceedings where the Probate Code does not otherwise provide an applicable rule (see Probate Code sec. 1000). A summons may be required in connection with specific probate proceedings, such as will contests (Prob. C. sec. 8250). But there is no general requirement to serve a summons with a probate petition, as there is to serve a summons with a civil complaint. A probate petition is not procedurally a civil complaint.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE IN RE: RICHARD YOUNG 2025 TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 4 Demurrer] The Demurrer also argues the Cross-Petition's inclusion of Donna Lew and Martha Ly is an invalid joinder, under Code of Civil Procedure sections 428.20 and 379. Despite the Notice of Demurrer stating Dykas, Lew, and Ly were specially appearing together on this demurrer, the Memorandum of Points and Authorities focuses on the pleading's deficiencies specifically as to the factual allegations about Tina Dykas, and does not specifically argue deficiencies as to the pleading's allegations against Respondents Ly or Lew, except as to their joinder in the Cross-Petition. The court rules on the Demurrer accordingly. Demurrer to the First, Second, Third, Fifth, Sixth, and Seventh cause of action on the basis of lack of standing is OVERULED. The Cross-Petition states either directly or by incorporation that Calica is a beneficiary of the estate, an interested party to the estate, and the successor trustee to the related Trust (see Cross-Petition at par. 4, 20, 23, 48, 82, 94, 97, 102, 105). Demurrer to the Fourth Cause of Action on the basis of lack of standing is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. The Fourth cause of action for Elder Physical Abuse and Neglect does not plead any facts regarding Calica's standing to bring the claim. Demurrer to the First Cause of Action for Disinheritance of an Heir Under Probate Code sec. 259 et seq. Declaratory Relief Against Tina Dykas, on the basis of failure to state sufficient facts to plead the necessary elements of a claim under Probate Code section 259, as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Demurrer to the Second Cause of Action for Isolation of an Elder (Elder Abuse) Welf. & Inst. Code secs. 15610.07, 15610.43, 15657.5, on the basis of failure to state sufficient facts to plead the elements of "abuse" as defined under Section 15610.07 and "abduction" as referenced in Section 15657.5 and defined in Section 15610.06, as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Demurrer to the Third Cause of Action for Financial Elder Abuse (Welf & Inst. C. sec. 15610.30), on the basis of failure to state sufficient facts to plead the elements of "financial abuse" as defined under Section 15610.30, as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Demurrer to the Fourth Cause of Action for Physical Elder Abuse (Violation of Welf & Inst. C. secs. 15610.07, 15610.63, 15657), on the basis of failure to state sufficient facts to plead the elements of "abuse" as defined under Section 15610.07, "physical abuse" as defined under Section 15610.63, neglect as defined in Section 15610.57, or abandonment as defined in Section 15610.05" referenced in Section 15657, as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE IN RE: RICHARD YOUNG 2025 TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioner Tina Dykas And Respondents Martha Ly And Donna Lew Notice Of Demurrer And Demurrer To Defendant Respondent Cross-Petitioner Elisa Calica?S Amended Cross-Petition
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 4 Demurrer] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Stephen M. Murphy. Before the court is Petitioner Tina Dykas's Demurrer To Defendant Respondent Cross-Petitioner Elisa Calica's Amended Cross-Petition ("Demurrer"). The court notes the Notice of Demurrer states, Petitioner/Cross-Respondent Tina Dykas and Respondents Martha Ly and Donna Lew, all by limited/special appearance together with their simultaneous motion to squash [sic]/motion to strike, will demurrer [sic]to Defendant/Respondent/Cross-Petitioner Elisa Calica's Amended Cross-Petition and the FIRST, SECOND, THIRD, FOURTH, FIFTH, SIXTH, SEVENTH and EIGHTH Causes of Action therein and to the inclusion of Donna Lew and Martha Ly as Respondents therein, generally and specifically as follows:[] Notice at 1:10-17. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 1014, "A defendant appears in an action when the defendant answers, demurs, files a notice of motion to strike[.]" Respondents Ly and Lew have filed at least one of those pleadings. Dykas is the initial Petitioner in this matter. The case Respondents cite in the concurrently filed Motion to Strike/Motion to Quash, Parsons v. Superior Ct. (2007) 149 Cal. App. 4th Supp. 1, 5 arose from service of summons in an unlawful detainer action, and the portion of the opinion Respondents rely on was overruled by Borsuk v. App. Div. of Superior Ct. (2015) 242 Cal. App. 4th 607. The court finds Dykas, Ly, and Lew have now appeared in this matter. On July 7, 2025, Elisa Calica filed Amended Cross-Petition 1. Disinheritance of an Heir Under California Probate Code 259 et seq; Declaratory Relief; 2. Isolation of an Elder Under Cal. W&I Code 15610.43; 3. Elder Financial Abuse; 4. Elder Physical Abuse and Neglect; 5. Recovery of Real and Personal Property to be Declared Part of Trust Pursuant to Probate Code 850; 6. Negligence/Negligence Per Se; 8. [sic] Indemnity ("Cross-Petition"). On July 14, 2025, Amendment to Amended Cross-Petition to Include Verification was filed. The Demurrer was filed July 17, 2025. A demurrer is a pleading used to challenge the legal sufficiency of an opponent's pleading, based on defects that appear either on the face of the pleading under attack or from matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable. (See Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318; County of Fresno v. Shelton (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 996, 1008, 1009.) When considering a demurrer, the trial court must accept as true all material facts pleaded in the complaint and those arising by reasonable implication. (Moore v. Conliffe (1994) 7 Cal. 4th 634, 638; Montclair Parkowners Ass'n v. City of Montclair (1999) 76 Cal. App. 4th 784.; Sheehan v. San Francisco 49ers, Ltd. (2009) Cal.4th 992, 998.) A ruling on a demurrer does not determine contested factual issues relating to the merits. Per the Notice of Demurrer, Petitioner demurs to the First, Second, Third, Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth cause of action pleaded in the Cross-Petition, under Code of Civil Procedure sections 430.10(e) and (f) - lack of standing to assert the claim, and insufficient facts pleaded to constitute a cause of action and/or the pleading is uncertain.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE IN RE: RICHARD YOUNG 2025 TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2 Motion to Strike/Motion to Quash] The Probate Code's general notice provisions are under Probate Code sec. 1200 et seq. ("Except as otherwise provided in this code, this part governs notice required or permitted under this code. This part does not apply to notice under a particular provision to the extent that the particular provision is inconsistent with this part." Section 1200.) Under Probate Code sec. 1215, "Unless otherwise expressly provided, a notice or other paper that is required or permitted to be delivered to a person shall be delivered" by personal service, first-class mail, or electronic service. There are, also, statutory rules of procedure specific to certain types of petitions brought under certain portions of the Probate Code such a Section 850 et seq. Respondents argue they were not properly served under Probate Code section 851 because they were not personally served copies of the Cross-Petition and a summons. Under Section 851(a), the petitioner must serve notice of the hearing on the petition and a copy of the petition in the manner provided in Code of Civil Procedure section 413.10 et seq, on the personal representative, conservator, guardian, or trustee "as appropriate" and on each person claiming an interest in the property or having title or possession. If the matter concerns a trust, notice of the hearing and a copy of the petition must also be served as provided in Probate Code section 17203 on each person listed in Section 17203. (Prob. C. sec. 851(b)(3).) Notice of the hearing must be served at least 30 days before the day of the hearing (Prob. C. sec. 851(a)). The notice for a petition brought under Probate Code section 851 is given on the general Judicial Council form DE-120, "Notice of Hearing-Decedent's Estate or Trust." The manner of service of the Notice of Hearing and copy of the petition may be by personal delivery, mail, substituted service, or by publication, by an authorized person, if served within the state, or by any of those manners or by first class mail with a return receipt if serve outside the state (see, Code of Civil Proc. secs. 414.10, 415.10 et seq, 415.40). No summons is required, under the Probate Code. Currently, the Cross-Petition is on for hearing on September 2, 2025. There is currently no Notice of Hearing on file for the Cross-Petition. If proof of proper service and required mailed notice pursuant to Probate Code section 851 is not received by the court timely and to the court's satisfaction, hearing on the Cross-Petition will be continued on the court's own motion, per this court's usual procedure. Elisa Calica shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE IN RE: RICHARD YOUNG 2025 TRUST TRUST
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR For The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 4 Demurrer] Demurrer to the Fifth Cause of Action for Recovery of Real and Personal Property Inadvertently Excluded from Trust Pursuant to Probate Code sec. 850 (Heggstad Petition), on the basis of failure to state sufficient facts to plead the cause of action as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. The Cross-Petition does not plead facts stating Dykas' relationship to the property at issue in the Heggstad petition. Demurrer to the Sixth Cause of Action for Elder Abuse and Neglect, on the basis of failure to plead sufficient facts to state a cause of action as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. The Cross-Petition's statement "upon information and belief" that Dykas "may have overmedicated Mr. Young or failed to administer the medications he required" is insufficient to plead the elements of "elder abuse" as defined under Welf & Inst. Code sec. 15610.07(a)(1), "physical abuse" as defined under Welf & Inst. Code sec. 15610.63, or "neglect" as defined under Welf & Inst. Code sec. Demurrer to the Seventh Cause of Action for Negligence/Negligence Per Se, , on the basis of failure to plead sufficient facts to state a cause of action as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Demurrer to the Eighth Cause of Action for Indemnity, on the basis of failure to state a cause of action as to Tina Dykas, is SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Demurrer to "joinder" of Respondents Ly and Lew is OVERULED . While civil action rules are applicable in Probate proceedings where the Probate Code does not otherwise provide an applicable rule (see Probate Code sec. 1000), proceedings brought under the Probate Code are not "civil actions" per se. "A civil action is prosecuted by one party against another for the declaration, enforcement or protection of a right, or the redress or prevention of a wrong" and is commenced by filing a complaint with the court (Code of Civil Proc. secs. 30, 411.10.) Indeed, the Probate court, on its own motion, may order that an action or proceeding not specifically provided in the Probate Code be determined in a separate civil action and may order transfer of the severed action or proceeding to the separate civil action (Prob. C. sec. 801). In Probate proceedings, the operative pleading is a petition. There are statutory rules of procedure specific to certain types of petitions brought under certain portions of the Probate Code (e.g., a petition brought pursuant to Section 17200 et seq, or Section 850 et seq). Unlike a civil action, a probate petition is not necessarily an adversarial pleading. While there may be competing claims or petitions in a probate proceeding, there is no such thing as a "cross-petition" under the Probate Code. The civil rules regarding joinder in cross-complaints (see, Code of Civil Proc. sec 428.10 et seq) are not applicable to probate proceedings. Insofar as the Cross-Petition alleges Respondents Dykas, Ly, and Lew acted in concert and/or are jointly liable for the alleged harms, and to the extent the Probate Code does not otherwise provide an applicable rule, it appears correct at this early stage of proceedings for them to be named as respondents in a single pleading (see Code of Civil Proc. sec 379).
